1.8.a.info:
An IPv6 address is exactly 128 bits long, four times the size of IPv4's 32 bits. That creates an address space of 2^128 ≈ 340 undecillion (3.4 × 10^38) unique addresses — enough to assign roughly 6.67 × 10^17 addresses to every square millimeter on Earth's surface. This is not marketing hyperbole; it is why SLAAC can afford to burn half of every address (64 bits) on the interface ID.

Why 128 bits? When IPv6 was designed in the 1990s, the IETF chose 128 bits specifically to allow hierarchical allocation with massive waste built in. ISPs get /32s (2^96 addresses — 79 octillion each), enterprises get /48s, LAN segments get /64s. Every level can waste 99.9% of its address space and still never run out. This is the opposite philosophy from IPv4, where every /30 is hoarded.

Notation: Because writing 128 bits in binary is unreadable (128 ones and zeros), IPv6 uses hexadecimal — each hex digit represents 4 bits, so 128 bits = 32 hex digits grouped into 8 hextets of 4 digits each. Example full form: 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. Each colon separates a 16-bit hextet.

Exam trap: Students often answer 64 bits because so much emphasis is placed on the /64 LAN prefix. The full address is always 128 bits — /64 is just the standard split between network and host, not the total length. If you see a question asking the length of an IPv6 address, the answer is 128.

params.bits: 128 bits total = 8 hextets × 16 bits = 32 hex digits
params.label: IPv6 address length

hack.memory: IPv6 = 128 bits. Four times IPv4. Think: 32 × 4 = 128. 2^128 is so big that every grain of sand on Earth could have a billion addresses. Never confuse /64 (the LAN prefix) with the total length (128).
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'IPv6 address length?' → 128 bits → 32 hex characters → 8 hextets. Write out 3 full IPv6 addresses without shortening and count — each should have exactly 32 hex digits.
hack.meta: Guaranteed 1-question topic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Common trap: answer choices include 32 (IPv4), 64 (LAN prefix), 128 (correct), 256 (wrong) — pick 128.

1.8.a.info:
A hextet (also called a quartet or 16-bit group) is one of the eight 16-bit groups in an IPv6 address, written as up to 4 hexadecimal digits and separated from other hextets by colons. In the address 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001, each of the 8 colon-separated groups is a hextet.

Why 4 hex digits = 16 bits: Each hex digit represents 4 bits (0–F covers 16 values = 2^4). Four hex digits × 4 bits = 16 bits per hextet. Eight hextets × 16 bits = 128 bits total.

Value range per hextet: 0000 to FFFF, which in decimal is 0 to 65,535. This is why the subnet ID field in a /48 organization allocation (bits 49–64) gives exactly 65,536 possible /64 subnets.

Hextet examples: 2001 (first hextet of documentation range), DB8 (second hextet when leading zero is dropped from 0DB8), FE80 (first hextet of link-local), FFFF (maximum value — often seen in EUI-64's FFFE insertion or in IPv4-mapped addresses).

Terminology note: Cisco documentation uses hextet; RFCs sometimes say 16-bit group. Both mean the same thing. The exam may use either.

hack.memory: Hextet = HEX group of 4 digits = 16 bits. 8 hextets, 8 colons... wait, only 7 colons separate 8 hextets. A full address has 7 colons (count them: A:B:C:D:E:F:G:H = 7 separators).
hack.practice: Count hextets in 5 sample addresses. Confirm each has exactly 8 groups of up to 4 hex digits. Convert one hextet (e.g., DB8) to decimal: D=13, B=11, 8=8 → 13×256 + 11×16 + 8 = 3512.
hack.meta: Vocabulary-level question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Know the synonyms: hextet = quartet = 16-bit group. The exam may say 'how many hextets in an IPv6 address?' → 8.

1.8.a.info:
The IPv6 header is a fixed 40 bytes, dramatically simpler than IPv4's variable 20–60 byte header. This simplification was a deliberate design goal — it makes router forwarding faster because the parser knows exactly where every field lives, with no options to walk past.

What IPv6 keeps: Version (4 bits), Traffic Class (8 bits, like IPv4 ToS/DSCP), Flow Label (20 bits, for flow-based QoS), Payload Length (16 bits), Next Header (8 bits, replaces Protocol), Hop Limit (8 bits, replaces TTL), Source Address (128 bits), Destination Address (128 bits). Total: exactly 40 bytes.

What IPv6 removed:

  • Header Checksum — gone. Layer 2 (Ethernet FCS) and Layer 4 (TCP/UDP checksums) already verify integrity. Routers no longer recompute a checksum on every hop.
  • Fragmentation fields (Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset) — moved to an optional Fragment extension header. Only the source can fragment in IPv6; routers never fragment (they send ICMPv6 Packet Too Big instead).
  • Options / Padding — moved to extension headers (Hop-by-Hop, Routing, Fragment, Authentication, ESP, Destination Options). This keeps the main header fixed-size.
  • IHL (Internet Header Length) — unnecessary because the header is always 40 bytes.

Why this matters for routers: A router's fast path (ASIC or CEF) can parse an IPv6 header in one memory read because every field is at a known offset. IPv4's variable options field forced routers to walk a variable-length region, slowing per-packet processing. IPv6 is faster per packet at the cost of longer addresses.

hack.memory: IPv6 header = fixed 40 bytes, lean and mean. Killed: checksum, options, router fragmentation. Mnemonic: 'Four zero, nothing to know' — 40 bytes and everything's at a predictable offset. TTL became Hop Limit. Protocol became Next Header. Options moved out entirely.
hack.practice: Flashcard the 8 IPv6 header fields in order. Compare side by side with the 13 IPv4 fields and mark which were removed or renamed. In Wireshark, capture an IPv6 ping and expand the header — verify you see exactly the 8 fields listed above and count 40 bytes.
hack.meta: Common exam question: 'What size is the IPv6 header?' = 40 bytes fixed. 'What was removed from IPv6 headers?' = checksum, fragmentation fields, options. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22.

1.8.a.info:
IPv6 has no broadcast address. There is no equivalent of 255.255.255.255 or subnet directed broadcasts. Instead, IPv6 uses multicast (one-to-many, joined groups only) and anycast (one-to-nearest) to cover everything broadcast used to handle.

Why this is an upgrade: Broadcasts are wasteful — every NIC on a segment must process the frame up to L3, even if the payload is irrelevant. On large flat networks with thousands of hosts, broadcast storms from ARP, DHCP, NetBIOS, and mDNS drown CPUs and saturate bandwidth. IPv6 multicast restricts traffic to devices that explicitly joined a group, dramatically reducing CPU waste on uninterested hosts.

Replacements for common IPv4 broadcast use cases:

  • ARP request (broadcast): Replaced by NS (Neighbor Solicitation) sent to the solicited-node multicast address FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx — only the target host (and collisions, rare) listens.
  • All-hosts broadcast: Replaced by FF02::1 (all-nodes link-local multicast). Every IPv6 node joins automatically.
  • DHCP Discover (broadcast 255.255.255.255): Replaced by DHCPv6 Solicit to FF02::1:2 (all DHCPv6 servers/relays).
  • Routing protocol hellos: OSPFv3 uses FF02::5 and FF02::6; EIGRP for IPv6 uses FF02::A.

Exam impact: Any answer choice that includes 'IPv6 broadcast' is wrong by definition. Watch for trick questions like 'which address does a host use to reach all nodes on the link?' → FF02::1, not a broadcast.

hack.memory: IPv6 = no broadcast, ever. Multicast replaces the yelling-at-everyone model with a subscription model — only devices that joined the group process the frame. Any answer saying 'IPv6 broadcast' is automatically wrong.
hack.practice: Make a table: IPv4 broadcast use → IPv6 replacement. ARP → NS to solicited-node. DHCP Discover → Solicit to FF02::1:2. All-hosts → FF02::1. This mapping is the fastest way to retain it.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Phrase cold: 'IPv6 replaces broadcast with multicast.'

1.8.a.info:
SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) is a native IPv6 feature that lets a host generate its own valid global IPv6 address without any DHCP server. It is arguably the most valuable user-facing improvement over IPv4, where DHCP is mandatory for dynamic addressing.

High-level process: The host sends a Router Solicitation (RS) to FF02::2 asking any router for a prefix. The router replies with a Router Advertisement (RA) containing the /64 prefix, default gateway (the router's link-local), and lifetime. The host combines the /64 prefix with a self-generated 64-bit interface ID (via EUI-64 or random/RFC 7217) to form a complete 128-bit address, runs DAD (Duplicate Address Detection), and starts using it.

Zero DHCP server required for addressing. Stateless because no server keeps a binding table. Every host on a /64 picks its own interface ID from the 2^64 possible values — collision probability is astronomically small.

Requirement — /64 prefix: SLAAC only works on /64 LAN segments because the interface ID must be exactly 64 bits. If a router advertises a /56 or /80, hosts cannot run SLAAC. This is why /64 is the non-negotiable standard LAN size in IPv6.

DNS gap: Classic SLAAC provides the address and default gateway but no DNS server. Solutions: stateless DHCPv6 (O-flag=1, host queries DHCPv6 for DNS only) or the RDNSS option in the RA (RFC 8106, newer but widely supported).

hack.memory: SLAAC = Self-Leased Address Assignment (Claude's mnemonic). Host asks 'who's the router here?' (RS), router answers 'I am, here's the prefix' (RA), host invents its own interface ID, done. No DHCP, no state, no drama. Requires /64.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, enable ipv6 unicast-routing on R1 and assign 2001:DB8:1::1/64 to a LAN. Connect a PC set to auto-config. Run ipconfig on the PC — note both a link-local and a 2001:DB8:1:: global address generated without any DHCP.
hack.meta: Core IPv6 concept. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: SLAAC is stateless, requires /64, and does not provide DNS by itself.

1.8.a.info:
The 64/64 split is the universal IPv6 LAN convention: the first 64 bits identify the network prefix, and the last 64 bits identify the interface ID (the host). This split is hardcoded in SLAAC and baked into nearly every IPv6 deployment guide.

Why 64 bits for the host? It lets the interface ID be derived from a 48-bit MAC via EUI-64 (MAC → insert FFFE in middle → flip 7th bit → 64 bits) and gives each LAN 2^64 ≈ 1.8 × 10^19 possible hosts — more than the number of grains of sand on Earth. Collisions between randomly chosen interface IDs are statistically impossible.

Why 64 bits for the network? ISPs hand out /32 to /48 blocks; organizations subnet those into /64s. Even a small /48 (standard site allocation) contains 65,536 /64 LANs — more than any enterprise needs.

Concrete example: address 2001:0DB8:CAFE:0001:021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E/64. Network prefix = 2001:0DB8:CAFE:0001::/64. Interface ID = 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E (EUI-64 generated from MAC 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E).

Exceptions — when /64 is not used:

  • /127 on point-to-point router-to-router links (RFC 6164 recommends this — 2 addresses, no waste)
  • /128 for loopback interfaces or specific host routes
  • /48, /56 at the allocation level (not on individual LAN segments)

On every end-user LAN, VLAN, or wireless SSID, the prefix is /64. Period.

hack.memory: 64 up top (network), 64 down below (host). Every LAN is /64. Mnemonic: 'Sixty-four on the floor' — remember it, you'll get every SLAAC/LAN/subnet question right. The 64/64 split also means subnet math on CCNA is easy: you almost never compute it.
hack.practice: Given 2001:DB8:CAFE:0001::/64, identify the network (first 64 bits) and the host portion (last 64 bits) of a full address. Practice with 5 random EUI-64 derived addresses.
hack.meta: Foundational concept. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Know: /64 is required for SLAAC; every LAN uses /64.

1.8.a.info:
IPv6 exists because IPv4 ran out of public addresses. IPv4's 32-bit space allows ~4.3 billion addresses (2^32). Designed in 1981 for a research network, it was never planned for a world with 30+ billion connected devices.

Timeline of exhaustion:

  • Feb 2011: IANA handed out the last five /8 blocks to the RIRs (one per region). This was 'IANA exhaustion.'
  • Apr 2011: APNIC (Asia-Pacific) exhausted its general pool.
  • Sep 2012: RIPE (Europe) exhausted.
  • Jun 2014: LACNIC (Latin America) exhausted.
  • Sep 2015: ARIN (North America) exhausted.
  • Nov 2019: RIPE assigned its final /22 — full exhaustion.

NAT as a band-aid: NAT (Network Address Translation) extended IPv4 by letting many private hosts share one public address. This works but breaks end-to-end connectivity: peer-to-peer apps, VoIP, gaming, IPsec, and server hosting all require workarounds (STUN, TURN, hole punching, UPnP, port forwarding). CGNAT (carrier-grade NAT) at ISPs adds a second NAT layer, making diagnosis even harder.

IPv6 fixes the structural issue, not just the count. 340 undecillion addresses mean every device gets a globally routable public address. NAT becomes optional, end-to-end connectivity returns, and security policies can use actual endpoint addresses instead of rewritten ones. Additional wins: simpler header, no broadcast, built-in SLAAC, required IPsec (in the original spec), and multicast scopes.

hack.memory: IPv4 = 4.3 billion addresses, gone by 2011. IPv6 = 340 undecillion, won't run out. NAT kept IPv4 alive but broke peer-to-peer. IPv6 restores end-to-end connectivity.
hack.practice: Quick quiz: when did IANA exhaust? Feb 2011. Why is NAT a workaround rather than a fix? It breaks end-to-end connectivity. What does IPv6 remove the need for? NAT.
hack.meta: Background/context question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. The exam may mention IPv4 exhaustion or NAT-traversal pain as the motivation for IPv6.

1.8.b.info:
Rule 1 of IPv6 shortening: within any hextet, remove leading zeros (zeros at the beginning of the group). You must keep at least one digit per hextet, so a group that is all zeros becomes 0, not empty. Trailing and embedded zeros stay.

Per-hextet examples:

  • 0DB8 → DB8 (drop one leading zero)
  • 0001 → 1 (drop three leading zeros)
  • 0000 → 0 (drop three, keep one — the hextet still exists)
  • 00AB → AB (drop two leading zeros)
  • DB80 → DB80 (unchanged — trailing zeros are not leading)
  • 0D0E → D0E (drop leading zero; embedded zero stays)

Full address example: 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 after Rule 1 only → 2001:DB8:0:0:0:0:0:1. Notice the colons and count of hextets are preserved — Rule 1 shortens within each group but does not merge groups. Merging is Rule 2's job.

Common mistake: deleting a trailing zero. DB80 is not the same as DB8. The value DB80 = 56,192 decimal; DB8 = 3,512 decimal. They differ by a factor of 16. Only drop leading zeros.

Reverse (expanding): when you see DB8, pad it back to 4 digits with leading zeros → 0DB8. Expansion is always exactly 4 hex digits per hextet.

hack.memory: Kill leading zeros only. 0DB8→DB8. 0001→1. 0000→0 (never empty). DB80 stays DB80 because those zeros are trailing. Keep at least one digit per hextet — it's still a valid group.
hack.practice: Take 10 hextets with leading zeros and shorten each. Then reverse — expand 10 shortened hextets back to 4 digits. The reverse direction is tested just as often.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam mechanic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Common trick: an answer drops trailing zeros — automatically wrong.

1.8.b.info:
Rule 2: a single consecutive run of all-zero hextets can be replaced by :: (two colons). The :: represents one or more groups of 0000. This is the dramatic shortening most people think of when they see IPv6.

Applying Rule 2 after Rule 1:

  • 2001:DB8:0:0:0:0:0:1 → 2001:DB8::1 (six zeros collapsed)
  • FE80:0:0:0:20C:29FF:FE12:3456 → FE80::20C:29FF:FE12:3456 (three zeros collapsed)
  • 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1 → ::1 (loopback)
  • 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:0 → :: (unspecified)
  • FF02:0:0:0:0:0:0:1 → FF02::1 (all-nodes multicast)

Must be consecutive and all-zero: 2001:DB8:0:1:0:0:0:1 cannot collapse the first 0 because it is a single zero between non-zeros. Only the run of three zeros in positions 5-7 can collapse → 2001:DB8:0:1::1. You cannot break a non-zero hextet with ::.

Pick the longest run: If an address has two separate runs of zeros, :: goes on the longer one. If they are equal length, convention says use the leftmost. Example: 2001:0:0:1:0:0:0:1 has a run of 2 and a run of 3 — use :: on the longer: 2001:0:0:1::1.

Valid address with just ::: by itself, :: means all-zeros (the unspecified address). ::1 is the loopback. Both are legal and common in IPv6.

hack.memory: :: = double colon, one run of zeros gone. Only consecutive all-zero groups. Pick the longest run. :: alone = all zeros (unspecified). ::1 = loopback.
hack.practice: Shorten 10 full addresses with both rules. Then expand 10 shortened ones. If you see :: applied in the middle of non-zero hextets, reject that answer.
hack.meta: Tested on every IPv6 exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22.

1.8.b.info:
The double-colon :: can appear only once per address. This restriction exists because :: represents a variable number of zero hextets — if it appeared twice, there would be no way to know how many zeros each instance represents.

The ambiguity problem: imagine 2001::1::5. The address must total 8 hextets. You have 2001, 1, and 5 — 3 visible hextets. The 5 missing zero hextets could be split as 1+4, 2+3, 3+2, or 4+1 between the two :: instances. There is no way to choose. So the rule: only one ::.

When an address has two separate runs of zeros, pick the longer:

  • 2001:0:0:1:0:0:0:1 → runs of 2 and 3 → use :: on the longer → 2001:0:0:1::1, not 2001::1:0:0:0:1
  • 2001:0:0:1:0:0:1:1 → both runs are 2 → by convention use the leftmost → 2001::1:0:0:1:1
  • 2001:1:0:0:1:0:0:1 → both runs of 2 → leftmost → 2001:1::1:0:0:1

Expanding addresses with one ::: count visible hextets, subtract from 8, fill :: with that many 0000 groups. Example: 2001:DB8::1 has 3 visible → 8 − 3 = 5 zero groups → 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001.

Exam implication: any answer with two :: is wrong by syntax. Always check the syntax validity first before thinking about the value.

hack.memory: Two colons, one time. Two :: = ambiguous = invalid. If you spot two :: in an answer choice, eliminate it immediately.
hack.practice: Spot-check 20 candidate IPv6 addresses for syntax. Any with zero :: and fewer than 8 hextets? Invalid. Any with two ::? Invalid. Any with :: and exactly one consecutive run of zeros? Valid.
hack.meta: Classic exam trap. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. 'Which of these IPv6 addresses is valid?' — always eliminate the two-:: option first.

1.8.b.info:
To expand a shortened IPv6 address back to its 128-bit form, reverse the two rules in the opposite order: first expand ::, then pad leading zeros.

Algorithm:

  1. Count the visible hextets (groups between colons, ignoring the ::).
  2. Subtract from 8 to get the number of zero hextets the :: represents.
  3. Replace :: with that many 0000 groups, joined by colons.
  4. Pad every visible hextet with leading zeros until each is 4 hex digits.

Example 1: 2001:DB8::1

  • Visible hextets: 2001, DB8, 1 → 3 visible
  • Zero hextets needed: 8 − 3 = 5
  • After expanding ::: 2001:DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:1
  • After padding: 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001

Example 2: FE80::20C:29FF:FE12:3456

  • Visible: FE80, 20C, 29FF, FE12, 3456 → 5 visible
  • Zero hextets: 8 − 5 = 3
  • Expanded: FE80:0000:0000:0000:020C:29FF:FE12:3456

Example 3: ::1

  • Visible: 1 → 1 visible
  • Zero hextets: 8 − 1 = 7
  • Expanded: 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001

Edge case — :: alone: :: has 0 visible hextets → 8 zero hextets → 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000.

hack.memory: Expand :: = (8 − visible) zero hextets. 2001:DB8::1 → 3 visible → 5 zeros → 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. Then pad each group to 4 digits.
hack.practice: Expand 10 random shortened addresses by hand. Write out the visible count, the 8 − visible subtraction, the replacement, and the final padded form. Verify with an online calculator.
hack.meta: Routine exam mechanic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22.

1.8.b.info:
:: by itself is the IPv6 unspecified address — all 128 bits are zero. It is the exact analogue of IPv4's 0.0.0.0 and is used in the same contexts.

Full form: 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000 / 128.

Where it appears:

  • DHCPv6 Solicit source: a host that does not yet have an address uses :: as its source when sending DHCPv6 messages.
  • DAD (Duplicate Address Detection): the Neighbor Solicitation for DAD uses :: as the source because the host has not yet claimed the address it is testing.
  • Static default routes: ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8::1 matches any destination, same as ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 in IPv4.
  • Socket bind: binding a server socket to :: means listen on all interfaces, same as binding to 0.0.0.0 in IPv4.

What it is NOT: :: is not loopback. It is not a valid destination on the wire. It cannot be assigned as an interface address. It only appears as a source or as a wildcard.

Key distinctions:

  • :: = unspecified (0.0.0.0 equivalent, all zeros)
  • ::1 = loopback (127.0.0.1 equivalent, last bit set)
  • ::/0 = default route (any destination)
  • ::/128 = the specific unspecified address as a route

left.label: IPv4
items: Used as DHCP source before address

hack.memory: :: alone = 0.0.0.0 of IPv6. All zeros. Source-only, never a destination. Used for DHCPv6 Solicit and DAD before a host has claimed an address.
hack.practice: Flashcard: :: means what? → unspecified address. When is it used? → as source during DHCPv6 Solicit or DAD. What's the default route in IPv6? → ::/0.
hack.meta: Simple concept but easy to confuse with ::1. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Remember: :: = unspecified, ::1 = loopback.

1.8.b.info:
::1 is the IPv6 loopback address — the equivalent of IPv4's 127.0.0.1. In expanded form it is 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 / 128. Every IPv6 stack has it built in, always reachable on the host itself.

Use cases:

  • Testing the local TCP/IP stack: ping6 ::1 (Linux) or ping ::1 (modern OSes).
  • Binding localhost-only services (databases, admin interfaces) so they are unreachable from the network.
  • Application-layer sanity tests that do not require a NIC.

Characteristics:

  • Assigned to the loopback interface (lo0 on Unix, the loopback pseudo-interface on Windows).
  • Prefix: ::1/128 — a single host address, not a range. IPv4 had an entire 127.0.0.0/8 block (16 million addresses); IPv6 gives loopback exactly one address.
  • Packets sent to ::1 never leave the host. They go down the TCP/IP stack and back up, staying entirely in kernel memory.
  • Not routable anywhere — routers must drop packets with source or destination ::1 if they arrive on an external interface.

Exam trap: mixing :: (unspecified, all zeros) with ::1 (loopback, last bit set). Read carefully. The question 'which IPv6 address is the loopback?' must be answered with ::1, not ::.

children.name: Used for local stack tests: ping ::1

hack.memory: ::1 = loopback = 127.0.0.1 of IPv6. Remember: :: alone = all zeros (unspecified), ::1 = last bit set (loopback). ONE dot in ::1, ONE loopback address.
hack.practice: On your Mac or Linux box, run ping6 ::1 — verify reply from ::1. Then try ping :: — it fails (unspecified is not a valid destination). This hands-on confirms the distinction.
hack.meta: Common exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Know both :: and ::1 cold.

1.8.c.info:
/64 is the universal IPv6 LAN prefix length. Every Ethernet segment, VLAN, and wireless SSID uses /64. This is not just convention — it is a requirement for SLAAC, because the interface ID produced by EUI-64 or RFC 7217 is exactly 64 bits long and must fit into the host portion.

Size of a /64: 2^64 ≈ 1.8 × 10^19 addresses per segment. For comparison, the entire IPv4 internet has 2^32 ≈ 4.3 × 10^9 addresses. A single IPv6 /64 LAN contains roughly 4 billion IPv4 internets worth of addresses. Waste is intentional.

Example usage:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1:10::1/64
 no shutdown

Hosts on that segment get addresses like 2001:DB8:1:10::/64 with any 64-bit interface ID they invent (EUI-64 or random).

Why /64 specifically?

  • EUI-64 converts a 48-bit MAC to a 64-bit interface ID — no other prefix length fits.
  • RFC 4291 mandates /64 for all unicast subnets except ::/128 and ::1/128.
  • Uniform subnet size simplifies operations, scripting, and firewall rules.
  • Prevents subnet-math errors that plagued IPv4.

Exam lock: if asked what LAN prefix length to use for IPv6, answer /64 every time. If a question says SLAAC is configured but hosts are not getting addresses, check the prefix — it must be /64.

hack.memory: /64 = the magic LAN number. Required by SLAAC. Waste half the address (64 host bits) because there are plenty. If the question asks LAN prefix length, answer /64 automatically.
hack.practice: Lab in Packet Tracer: try to configure SLAAC with a /80 prefix. The host will not auto-generate a global address. Switch to /64 and it works instantly. This proves the /64 requirement.
hack.meta: Universal IPv6 rule. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Know: /64 for LANs, required for SLAAC.

1.8.c.info:
A /48 is the standard IPv6 block an ISP assigns to an organization or site. It gives the site 16 bits of internal subnetting space (bits 49–64), producing 2^16 = 65,536 possible /64 LAN subnets. This is vastly more than any enterprise needs, which is the point — IPv6 deliberately over-allocates so organizations can build deep hierarchies without running out.

Structure of a /48 → /64 hierarchy:

  • First 48 bits (3 hextets): Global Routing Prefix — assigned by the ISP, fixed for the site. Example: 2001:0DB8:CAFE::/48.
  • Bits 49–64 (4th hextet): Subnet ID — the organization controls this. 16 bits → 65,536 subnets.
  • Last 64 bits: Interface ID — assigned per host.

Practical example: ISP gives Acme Corp 2001:DB8:CAFE::/48.

  • Engineering LAN: 2001:DB8:CAFE:0001::/64
  • Sales LAN: 2001:DB8:CAFE:0002::/64
  • Wireless Guest: 2001:DB8:CAFE:0010::/64
  • Point-to-point to R2: 2001:DB8:CAFE:FFFE::/127
  • Maximum subnet: 2001:DB8:CAFE:FFFF::/64

RIR policy: ARIN and RIPE typically allocate /32 to ISPs, who then hand out /48 to end-sites and /56 or /60 to residential customers. Some providers give residential users a full /48. Organizations with multiple geographic locations may get a /44 or /40 to subnet further by site.

Why 65,536 subnets is more than enough: most enterprises have dozens to hundreds of VLANs, not thousands. The /48 gives a huge safety margin for growth, mergers, and structured subnet planning (e.g., use the first hex digit of the subnet ID to encode building number).

hack.memory: /48 = site handoff from ISP. 16 bits of subnet space = 65,536 LANs. Mnemonic: '48 at the door (ISP handoff), 64 on the floor (per LAN).' You rarely need more than a /48.
hack.practice: Given 2001:DB8:CAFE::/48, write out 5 distinct /64 subnets. Identify which hextet you are varying. Calculate the total number of /64s available (2^16 = 65,536).
hack.meta: Common exam concept. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Know: /48 is the standard site size, yields 65,536 /64 subnets.

1.8.c.info:
/127 is the recommended prefix length for point-to-point links between routers (RFC 6164). It provides exactly 2 usable addresses, perfect for a transit link that will only ever have 2 endpoints. This is the IPv6 analogue of IPv4's /30, which also gives 2 usable addresses (4 total minus network and broadcast).

Why /127 instead of /64 on P2P links?

  • No SLAAC needed on router-to-router links — routers are manually configured, so the /64-for-SLAAC rule does not apply.
  • Prevents neighbor discovery exhaustion attack (RFC 6164 motivation). On a /64 P2P link, an attacker can scan enough addresses to overflow the neighbor cache and cause a denial of service. /127 leaves only 2 addresses, making the attack impossible.
  • Cleaner operationally — 2 addresses instead of 2^64 means no ambiguity about which addresses exist on the link.

Configuration example:

R1:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:0:FFFE::0/127

R2:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:0:FFFE::1/127

Difference from IPv4 /30: IPv4's /30 has 4 addresses (network, host1, host2, broadcast). /127 has only 2 addresses (both usable as hosts) — IPv6 has no broadcast and does not reserve the all-zeros host address. Both endpoints use one address each.

/126 is occasionally seen in older deployments (4 addresses, like /30). Modern best practice is /127 for two-router links.

hack.memory: /127 = IPv6's /30 for router-to-router links. Exactly 2 addresses. RFC 6164. No SLAAC on infrastructure links — so /64 rule does not apply.
hack.practice: Configure a /127 between two routers in Packet Tracer. Verify both interfaces come up/up. Use show ipv6 route to confirm the connected /127 and local /128 show up.
hack.meta: Tested at a conceptual level. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Know: /127 for P2P, /64 for LANs.

1.8.c.info:
/128 identifies a single IPv6 host — a prefix with all 128 bits specified leaves no room for any other addresses. It is the IPv6 equivalent of IPv4's /32.

Common uses:

  • Loopback interfaces on routers — interface Loopback0 with a /128 address gives the router a stable identifier for OSPFv3 router ID, BGP neighbor source, and management.
  • Local routes (L entries) in the routing table — when you configure a /64 on an interface, the router also installs a /128 for its specific address so packets to the router itself are processed locally.
  • Static host routes — pinning traffic for one specific address to a specific next-hop: ipv6 route 2001:DB8::5/128 2001:DB8:1::2.
  • Anycast deployments — the same /128 address is configured on multiple devices; the network routes to the nearest.

Example — loopback on a router:

interface Loopback0
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:0:0::1/128

This appears in show ipv6 route as:

L 2001:DB8::1/128 is directly connected, Loopback0

Why /128 matters on CCNA: understanding L routes (local /128s) is critical when reading show ipv6 route. Every time you see an L entry, it is a /128 pointing to the router's own interface address. If your router has 3 IPv6 interfaces configured, you will see 3 L /128 entries plus 3 C /64 (or whatever prefix) entries.

hack.memory: /128 = single host, IPv6's /32. Every router interface generates an L /128 in the routing table. Loopback interfaces also use /128.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure interface Loopback0 with a /128 address. Run show ipv6 route — verify the L /128 entry. Also look at the /128 L entries for physical interfaces.
hack.meta: Routine exam concept. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. /128 = host route, like IPv4 /32.

1.8.c.info:
One of the biggest practical advantages of IPv6 on the CCNA: you almost never perform subnet math. The /64 convention eliminates the variable-length subnet problems that dominate IPv4 study.

What IPv4 required you to master:

  • Calculate subnet ranges from variable masks (/25, /26, /27, /28, /29, /30)
  • Determine the network, broadcast, and usable host range for each subnet
  • Apply VLSM (Variable-Length Subnet Masks) to conserve addresses
  • Summarize routes across bit boundaries
  • Spot overlapping subnets

What IPv6 requires:

  • Know the universal /64 for LANs
  • Identify which hextet varies between adjacent subnets (usually bit 49–64 = 4th hextet in a /48 allocation)
  • Recognize /48, /64, /127, /128 by their typical use case

Why this works: IPv6 has so many addresses that waste is free. You never need to fit 30 hosts into a /27 to save addresses. Every LAN gets a /64 regardless of size. Point-to-point links get /127. Loopbacks get /128. That is the whole subnetting story.

When subnet math does appear:

  • Identifying the subnet a host belongs to: trim the address to the prefix length. Example: 2001:DB8:CAFE:1:1234::/64 belongs to 2001:DB8:CAFE:1::/64.
  • Recognizing solicited-node multicast: FF02::1:FF + last 24 bits of the unicast.
  • EUI-64 conversion of a MAC.

Exam implication: questions asking you to identify the subnet ID or the number of available subnets are straightforward compared to IPv4. If a question presents 8 overlapping prefixes and asks for the best match, that is longest-prefix-match, not subnet math per se.

hack.memory: IPv6 subnet math = 'just use /64.' No wildcard gymnastics, no VLSM puzzles. Every LAN is /64, P2P is /127, single host is /128. Done.
hack.practice: Quiz: LAN prefix? /64. Router-to-router P2P? /127. Loopback? /128. Site from ISP? /48. Confirm you can answer all four in under 5 seconds each.
hack.meta: Relief topic after IPv4 subnetting. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Remember: IPv6 CCNA rarely asks you to compute subnets.

1.8.d.info:
SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration, RFC 4862) is the IPv6 feature that lets a host create its own globally routable address without any DHCP server. The host combines the /64 prefix learned from a Router Advertisement with a self-chosen 64-bit interface ID (EUI-64 or RFC 7217 random). The result is a complete 128-bit address that is valid for global use.

Stateless means: no server keeps track of who got which address. No binding table, no leases, no reservations. Each host is responsible for picking a unique interface ID and running DAD to confirm uniqueness.

Full SLAAC flow:

  1. Host boots and generates a link-local address (FE80::/10 + interface ID) automatically — this always happens, SLAAC or not.
  2. Host sends RS (Router Solicitation, ICMPv6 Type 133) to FF02::2.
  3. Router replies with RA (Router Advertisement, ICMPv6 Type 134) containing: /64 prefix, default gateway (router's FE80:: address), flags (A=1 for SLAAC, M/O for DHCPv6 hints), MTU, lifetime.
  4. Host combines prefix + interface ID = candidate address.
  5. Host runs DAD by sending NS for its own tentative address. If no NA reply, address is unique.
  6. Host claims the address and adds the default route via the router's link-local.

When SLAAC is used: default on most Linux, macOS, Windows, iOS, Android, IoT devices for IPv6 addressing. It is the preferred method unless DHCPv6 is explicitly required by policy.

Exam keywords: stateless, no DHCP, RA provides prefix, host generates interface ID, requires /64.

hack.memory: SLAAC = Stateless, no server tracking. Router gives the prefix; host picks the interface ID. Requires /64. Default on nearly all modern client OSes.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, enable ipv6 unicast-routing, assign 2001:DB8:1::1/64 to a LAN, connect a PC set to autoconfig. Run ipconfig on the PC — confirm an auto-generated 2001:DB8:1:: address with no DHCP server present.
hack.meta: Core IPv6 topic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: SLAAC = stateless, needs /64, no DHCP.

1.8.d.info:
A Router Solicitation (RS) is an ICMPv6 Type 133 message that a host sends when it first connects to a network to ask: 'Are there any routers here? Send me an RA.' It is the trigger for faster network bootstrapping — without RS, the host would have to wait for the next periodic RA (up to 200 seconds by default).

Destination: FF02::2 — the all-routers multicast link-local address. Only routers listen on this group; other hosts ignore the frame. This is more efficient than IPv4's DHCP Discover broadcast, which every device must process.

Source: typically the host's link-local address (FE80::). If the host has not yet generated its link-local, it may use :: (unspecified) as the source.

Payload: minimal — just an ICMPv6 header plus optional options (source link-layer address, etc.). Size is tiny, a few dozen bytes.

When RS is sent:

  • Host boots or activates the interface (wake from sleep, cable plug-in, Wi-Fi connect).
  • Host detects that link-layer connectivity was restored after a loss.
  • Host needs to refresh configuration and does not want to wait for the next RA.

Response: every router on the link replies with an RA (not multicast-replicated, each router sends its own). Hosts prefer the first valid RA or the one with the longest matching prefix.

Exam spec: know the type number (133), the destination (FF02::2), and the purpose (trigger RA).

hack.memory: RS = Router Solicit = 'Roll call, routers!' ICMPv6 Type 133. Destination FF02::2 (all routers). Host sends when it wants an immediate RA.
hack.practice: Flashcard RS: Type=133, destination=FF02::2, purpose=ask for RA. In Wireshark, capture a Linux boot cycle — you should see exactly one RS before the first RA arrives.
hack.meta: Basic NDP fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Memorize: RS=133, FF02::2.

1.8.d.info:
A Router Advertisement (RA) is an ICMPv6 Type 134 message a router sends to inform hosts of network configuration. RAs are the backbone of IPv6 autoconfiguration — they carry everything a host needs to pick an address (via SLAAC) and find the default gateway.

When RAs are sent:

  • In response to an RS (unicast or multicast depending on the implementation)
  • Periodically as unsolicited announcements. Default interval on Cisco routers: random between 200 seconds (upper bound) and 0.5× that lower. Tunable with ipv6 nd ra interval.

Destination: typically FF02::1 (all-nodes) for periodic RAs; may be unicast to the soliciting host in response to an RS.

Source: the router's link-local address (FE80::). Hosts use this as the default gateway — not the router's global address.

Content of an RA:

  • Prefix and prefix length (e.g., 2001:DB8:1::/64)
  • Default router lifetime (0 = not a default router; non-zero = valid as gateway)
  • M-flag (Managed Address Configuration — use DHCPv6 for address)
  • O-flag (Other Configuration — use DHCPv6 for DNS etc.)
  • A-flag (Address Autoconfiguration — use SLAAC with this prefix)
  • L-flag (On-link — prefix is reachable without a router)
  • MTU option and various lifetimes
  • Optional RDNSS (Recursive DNS Server, RFC 8106)

Key exam fact: the default gateway is the router's link-local, learned from the RA source. Even if the host uses stateful DHCPv6 for addressing, the gateway still comes from the RA.

nodes: Router periodic timer or RS received

hack.memory: RA = Router Announcement = 'Here's my prefix and I am your gateway.' Type 134. Sent to FF02::1. Default gateway = RA source (router's FE80::). Periodic every ~200 seconds.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, run show ipv6 interface on a router — notice it lists RA interval, lifetime, and prefix advertised. Change the interval with ipv6 nd ra interval and verify.
hack.meta: Critical NDP concept. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: RA=134, carries prefix + flags + gateway, default gateway is the router's link-local.

1.8.d.info:
EUI-64 (Extended Unique Identifier, 64-bit) is the classic method to derive a 64-bit interface ID from a 48-bit MAC address. It was the original SLAAC interface ID scheme and is still tested heavily on the CCNA despite being replaced by RFC 7217 privacy addressing on modern OSes.

Three-step conversion:

  1. Split the 48-bit MAC in half: first 24 bits (OUI — vendor) and last 24 bits (device ID).
  2. Insert FFFE (16 bits) between the two halves, expanding from 48 to 64 bits.
  3. Flip the 7th bit (the Universal/Local bit) of the first byte. XOR with 0x02.

Worked example: MAC 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E

  • Split: 00:1A:2B | 3C:4D:5E
  • Stuff: 00:1A:2B:FF:FE:3C:4D:5E = 001A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E
  • Flip 7th bit of 00: 00000000 → 00000010 = 02 hex
  • Final: 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E

Combined with prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64, full address: 2001:DB8:1::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E.

Why the bit flip? In the EUI-64 spec, bit 7 of byte 1 is the U/L bit: 0 = Locally administered, 1 = Universally administered. MAC addresses reuse this bit with inverted semantics: 0 = Universal (burned-in), 1 = Local. Flipping it converts a burned-in MAC (bit 7 = 0) to the EUI-64 convention (bit 7 = 1).

Identifying EUI-64 interface IDs: presence of FF:FE in the middle (bits 25–40 of the interface ID) is a giveaway.

hack.memory: EUI-64 = Split, Stuff, Flip. Split MAC in half, stuff FFFE in the middle, flip the 7th bit (XOR first byte with 0x02). 00→02, AA→A8, 1A→18. Look for FFFE in the middle to spot an EUI-64 ID.
hack.practice: Do 5 EUI-64 conversions by hand: MAC→interface ID. Verify with an online calculator. Also practice reverse: given 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E, what was the original MAC? Flip first byte back (02→00), remove FFFE → 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Master the XOR-with-0x02 shortcut for the 7th-bit flip.

1.8.d.info:
Privacy extensions (RFC 4941 and RFC 7217) generate a randomized interface ID instead of the MAC-derived EUI-64 value. This prevents an observer from tracking a device across networks by its constant EUI-64 interface ID.

The privacy problem with EUI-64: if your laptop's interface ID is always 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E (derived from the burned-in MAC), any website or network you visit can correlate sessions by that ID. Connecting to airport Wi-Fi, then home, then office — all three networks see the same interface ID, making you uniquely trackable.

Two approaches:

  • RFC 4941 (Temporary addresses): host generates random interface IDs that rotate periodically (hours to days). Old addresses remain valid briefly to avoid breaking active connections. Outgoing connections prefer the newest temporary address.
  • RFC 7217 (Stable randomized): host generates a pseudo-random interface ID that is stable for a given (prefix, interface) combination but changes when either changes. Combines privacy (no MAC leak) with stability (same LAN = same address across reboots).

Default behavior today:

  • Windows 10/11: uses both RFC 4941 (temporary) and RFC 7217 (stable) by default
  • macOS, iOS: RFC 7217 default, RFC 4941 temporary supplement
  • Linux (modern): RFC 7217 default, can enable RFC 4941
  • Android: RFC 7217 default

CCNA context: you still need to know EUI-64 conversion for the exam, but understand that real-world SLAAC interfaces rarely show EUI-64 IDs on modern client devices. Routers and servers typically still use EUI-64 or static IDs.

hack.memory: Privacy extensions = random interface IDs to prevent MAC-based tracking. RFC 4941 rotates, RFC 7217 stable-per-prefix. Modern clients default to these, NOT EUI-64.
hack.practice: On your laptop, check ipconfig (Windows) or ifconfig (Linux/macOS) for IPv6 addresses. Look for an address without FFFE in the middle — that is a privacy-extension address. Look also for a temporary suffix.
hack.meta: Modern context. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Know privacy extensions exist and why, but exam still tests EUI-64 mechanics.

1.8.d.info:
DAD (Duplicate Address Detection, RFC 4862) is a mandatory IPv6 mechanism that verifies no other node on the link already uses an address before a host starts using it. Every IPv6 address — link-local, global, SLAAC-generated, DHCPv6-assigned, manually configured — must pass DAD before becoming active.

Process:

  1. Host has a tentative address it wants to use.
  2. Host sends an NS (Neighbor Solicitation, ICMPv6 Type 135) for its own tentative address.
  3. Source of the NS is :: (unspecified) — the host has not yet claimed any address to send from.
  4. Destination is the solicited-node multicast address derived from the tentative address (FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx).
  5. If any other node responds with an NA, the address is a duplicate — the host must abandon it and try another.
  6. If no response arrives within the DAD timer (default 1 second, configurable), the address is unique and moves from tentative to preferred.

Timing: a host typically waits ~1 second for a reply. During this window, the address appears as tentative in show ipv6 interface. Once DAD completes, it shows as [TENTATIVE] removed / [VALID].

Duplicate outcome: if DAD fails (another host responds), the address is abandoned. For manually configured addresses, the interface is placed in a disabled state for that address. For SLAAC addresses, the host typically picks a new random interface ID and retries.

Exam points:

  • DAD uses NS (not NA) with source :: (unspecified).
  • DAD runs on every IPv6 address before use.
  • DAD destination is the solicited-node multicast (FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx).

hack.memory: DAD = 'is this address taken?' Host NS for its own address with source ::. No reply = unique. Reply = duplicate, abandon. Runs on every address, every time.
hack.practice: Flashcard: DAD uses NS, source ::, destination solicited-node multicast. In Wireshark, capture a host joining an IPv6 network — the first packets from the host should be DAD NS messages.
hack.meta: Common NDP exam fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: DAD = NS with source :: and solicited-node destination.

1.8.d.info:
The A-flag (Address Autoconfiguration flag) is a bit in the RA's Prefix Information Option that tells hosts whether to use SLAAC with the included prefix. A=1 means use SLAAC; A=0 means the prefix is informational only (for on-link determination) and SLAAC should not derive an address from it.

Default value: A=1 on Cisco routers. You almost never disable it — if the RA is advertising a prefix but A=0, hosts will not form a SLAAC address.

Combined with M and O flags to determine addressing method:

  • A=1, M=0, O=0: Pure SLAAC. Host builds address from prefix + interface ID. No DHCPv6 interaction.
  • A=1, M=0, O=1: SLAAC for address, stateless DHCPv6 for DNS/other info.
  • A=0, M=1: Stateful DHCPv6 only. No SLAAC. Host gets address from DHCPv6 server.
  • A=1, M=1: Both SLAAC and stateful DHCPv6 may be used (dual addressing). Common for mixed environments.

Cisco commands:

  • Default: A-flag is ON implicitly when a prefix is advertised.
  • Disable SLAAC for a prefix: ipv6 nd prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64 no-autoconfig (clears A-flag).
  • No advertise prefix at all: ipv6 nd prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64 no-advertise.

Exam tip: memorize the A/M/O flag combinations. Each combination unambiguously dictates which addressing method is in use.

states: A=1, M=0, O=1 → SLAAC + stateless DHCPv6 DNS

hack.memory: A = Address via SLAAC. A=1 default. Combine with M (Managed = stateful DHCPv6) and O (Other = stateless DHCPv6 for DNS). Memorize: 000=SLAAC, 001=SLAAC+DNS, 010=stateful.
hack.practice: Draw a truth table with A, M, O on one axis and addressing method on the other. Fill in all 4 rows. Commit to memory.
hack.meta: Classic exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know all four A/M/O combinations.

1.8.e.info:
Stateless DHCPv6 is a hybrid addressing model where SLAAC handles address generation and DHCPv6 provides only supplementary configuration (DNS server, domain name, NTP, etc.). The DHCPv6 server does not assign or track addresses — it just answers information requests. That is why it is called stateless: no binding table, no leases, no address pool.

Triggered by: RA flags A=1, O=1, M=0. A=1 tells hosts to use SLAAC for addressing. O=1 tells hosts other configuration is available via DHCPv6 (meaning: go query a DHCPv6 server for DNS etc.). M=0 tells hosts not to use DHCPv6 for addressing.

Flow:

  1. Host sends RS to FF02::2.
  2. Router replies with RA: prefix, A=1, O=1, M=0.
  3. Host self-generates address via SLAAC.
  4. Host sends DHCPv6 Information-Request to FF02::1:2 (all DHCPv6 servers/relays).
  5. DHCPv6 server replies with DNS server addresses, domain name, any configured options — but no address.

Why it exists: original SLAAC had no way to deliver DNS server info. Stateless DHCPv6 filled that gap without reintroducing the complexity of per-host binding tables. RFC 8106 (RDNSS option in RAs) is a newer alternative that lets the RA carry DNS info directly, but stateless DHCPv6 is still common because many networks separate router and DNS management.

Server configuration on Cisco:

ipv6 dhcp pool STATELESS-POOL
 dns-server 2001:DB8::53
 domain-name example.com
!
interface Gi0/0
 ipv6 nd other-config-flag
 ipv6 dhcp server STATELESS-POOL

The ipv6 nd other-config-flag sets O=1 in outgoing RAs.

hack.memory: Stateless DHCPv6 = SLAAC for the address, DHCPv6 for the extras (DNS/domain/NTP). O-flag only. Server does NOT track addresses. Flags: A=1, O=1, M=0.
hack.practice: Draw the A/O/M truth table for stateless DHCPv6. Configure an ipv6 dhcp pool with dns-server and domain-name, apply it with ipv6 dhcp server, and set ipv6 nd other-config-flag. Verify with show ipv6 interface.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam topic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: O=1 triggers stateless DHCPv6.

1.8.e.info:
The O-flag (Other Configuration) is a single bit in the RA that tells hosts: 'There is more configuration available from a DHCPv6 server — go ask.' When O=1, hosts send a DHCPv6 Information-Request to retrieve options like DNS, domain name, SIP server, NTP, and vendor-specific data.

O-flag alone does NOT affect addressing. If A=1 and O=1 and M=0, hosts still get their address from SLAAC. O only signals 'other config is available.' This is the defining characteristic of stateless DHCPv6.

Combinations to memorize:

  • O=0, M=0 (A=1): Pure SLAAC. No DHCPv6 interaction at all. DNS must come from RA's RDNSS option or be manually configured.
  • O=1, M=0 (A=1): Stateless DHCPv6. SLAAC for address, DHCPv6 for DNS/other.
  • O=0, M=1: Stateful DHCPv6. DHCPv6 provides address AND everything else.
  • O=1, M=1: Redundant — if M=1, DHCPv6 already provides everything; O is implied. Uncommon.

Cisco command: ipv6 nd other-config-flag under the interface. This sets O=1 in outgoing RAs. Remove with no ipv6 nd other-config-flag.

Verification: show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 shows the current O-flag setting. Look for lines like 'ND advertised other config flag is TRUE.'

hack.memory: O = Other configuration. O=1 triggers a DHCPv6 query for DNS/domain. Does NOT affect addressing. Cisco command: ipv6 nd other-config-flag.
hack.practice: Configure ipv6 nd other-config-flag on a router interface. Run show ipv6 interface and confirm 'other config flag is TRUE.' Remove it and verify it flips back.
hack.meta: Common exam distinction. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. O=1 → stateless DHCPv6.

1.8.e.info:
FF02::1:2 is the well-known multicast address for all DHCPv6 servers and relay agents on the local link. DHCPv6 clients send Solicit, Request, and Information-Request messages to this group; servers and relays listen on it.

Address breakdown:

  • FF02: multicast with link-local scope (scope=2)
  • ::1:2: the group ID assigned to DHCPv6 servers/relays

Clients use this address for:

  • Solicit (stateful DHCPv6, client asks for address)
  • Information-Request (stateless DHCPv6, client asks for DNS/other info)
  • Initial discovery of a DHCPv6 server when no unicast address is known

Servers and relays listen on: FF02::1:2 (link-scope) and FF05::1:3 (site-scope, for cross-subnet relay).

Why a multicast instead of broadcast? IPv6 has no broadcast. Multicast restricts traffic to devices that joined the DHCPv6 server/relay group. Normal hosts on the link do not receive these packets.

Contrast with IPv4 DHCP: IPv4 clients broadcast DHCPDISCOVER to 255.255.255.255. IPv6 clients multicast DHCPv6 Solicit to FF02::1:2. More efficient, same purpose.

hack.memory: FF02::1:2 = DHCPv6 server multicast. Link-local scope (FF02). All DHCPv6 servers and relays listen. Clients send Solicit and Information-Request here.
hack.practice: Flashcard: FF02::1:2 is for what? → DHCPv6 servers and relays. Confirm you can recognize it alongside FF02::1 (all nodes), FF02::2 (all routers), FF02::5 (OSPF).
hack.meta: Occasional exam fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Memorize along with other well-known FF02:: addresses.

1.8.e.info:
RDNSS (Recursive DNS Server option, RFC 8106) is an option in the RA that lets a router directly tell hosts which DNS servers to use. It eliminates the need for stateless DHCPv6 in the common case where the only thing DHCPv6 was providing was DNS.

Why it was added: original SLAAC had no way to deliver DNS info. Networks had to deploy stateless DHCPv6 just to hand out a DNS server address. This added server infrastructure, configuration, and a second protocol to run. RDNSS folds the DNS info into the RA that hosts already receive.

Cisco configuration:

interface Gi0/0
 ipv6 nd ra dns server 2001:DB8::53 86400

The router advertises 2001:DB8::53 as a DNS server with lifetime 86400 seconds (24 hours).

Host support:

  • Linux (modern): NetworkManager/systemd-networkd reads RDNSS natively.
  • macOS, iOS: native support since macOS 10.7 / iOS 10.
  • Windows: RDNSS support added in Windows 10 Creators Update (2017). Earlier Windows versions ignored RDNSS and required DHCPv6 or manual DNS.
  • Android: native support since 4.x.

Comparison to stateless DHCPv6: RDNSS is simpler — one less protocol. Stateless DHCPv6 is more feature-rich (domain search, NTP, SIP servers) and better tooled. Many networks use both: RDNSS for DNS and DHCPv6 for richer options.

CCNA context: know that RDNSS exists as the modern alternative. The exam focuses on SLAAC / stateless DHCPv6 / stateful DHCPv6 more than RDNSS.

hack.memory: RDNSS = DNS via RA. Skips DHCPv6 entirely for DNS. RFC 8106. Configure with ipv6 nd ra dns server <addr> <lifetime>.
hack.practice: Low priority — just flashcard: RFC 8106, DNS in RA, modern alternative to stateless DHCPv6.
hack.meta: Background knowledge. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Exam rarely drills on RDNSS specifically, but know it exists.

1.8.e.info:
ipv6 nd other-config-flag is the Cisco interface command that sets O=1 in outgoing Router Advertisements. This is how you trigger stateless DHCPv6 on a given LAN — hosts receiving these RAs will query DHCPv6 for DNS and other config while still using SLAAC for addressing.

Full config sequence:

ipv6 dhcp pool STATELESS
 dns-server 2001:DB8::53
 dns-server 2001:DB8::54
 domain-name corp.example.com
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
 ipv6 nd other-config-flag
 ipv6 dhcp server STATELESS
 no shutdown

Three pieces: (1) a DHCPv6 pool with DNS/domain; (2) O-flag set on the interface so hosts know to ask; (3) DHCPv6 server enabled on the interface so it responds to Information-Requests.

Default behavior without the command: O=0. Hosts use pure SLAAC and have no way to learn DNS (unless RDNSS is also configured).

Related commands:

  • ipv6 nd managed-config-flag — sets M=1 for stateful DHCPv6
  • ipv6 nd ra dns server — adds RDNSS DNS to RA directly
  • ipv6 nd prefix ... no-autoconfig — clears A-flag for a specific prefix

Verification: show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 — look for 'ND advertised other config flag is TRUE.'

nodes: RA carries O=1 → hosts query DHCPv6

hack.memory: ipv6 nd other-config-flag = set O=1 in RA = trigger stateless DHCPv6. Companion: ipv6 nd managed-config-flag for M=1 (stateful). Verify with show ipv6 interface.
hack.practice: Build a lab with an ipv6 dhcp pool providing DNS. Apply ipv6 nd other-config-flag on the LAN interface. Connect a PC and confirm it gets address from SLAAC and DNS from DHCPv6.
hack.meta: Config-focused exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23.

1.8.f.info:
Stateful DHCPv6 is full, centralized IPv6 address assignment — functionally identical to DHCPv4 for IPv4. The DHCPv6 server allocates addresses from a pool, tracks which client has which address in a binding table, manages leases with timers, and provides DNS, domain, and other options. Stateful means: the server maintains state about every client.

Triggered by RA flag M=1: when a host receives an RA with M=1 (Managed Address Configuration), it sends a DHCPv6 Solicit to FF02::1:2 and negotiates its address via the SARR four-step exchange.

Why use stateful instead of SLAAC?

  • Tight control over which addresses exist and who has them — important for security policies, audit logs, and compliance.
  • Reservations — bind a specific DUID to a specific address, like DHCPv4 reservations.
  • Short leases to reclaim addresses from devices that leave the network.
  • Predictable addresses — you can guarantee a device always gets the same address.

Cisco configuration:

ipv6 dhcp pool STATEFUL
 address prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64 lifetime infinite infinite
 dns-server 2001:DB8::53
 domain-name corp.example.com
!
interface Gi0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
 ipv6 nd managed-config-flag
 ipv6 nd prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64 no-autoconfig
 ipv6 dhcp server STATEFUL

Note: no-autoconfig on the prefix clears the A-flag so hosts do NOT run SLAAC alongside. This forces pure stateful DHCPv6.

Binding table: show ipv6 dhcp binding displays current leases — client DUID, address assigned, lease remaining.

hack.memory: Stateful DHCPv6 = full DHCP for IPv6. Like DHCPv4 but with IPv6 addresses. M-flag triggers it. Server tracks binding: DUID → address → lease.
hack.practice: Configure a stateful DHCPv6 pool. Apply ipv6 nd managed-config-flag and ipv6 nd prefix ... no-autoconfig. Verify binding with show ipv6 dhcp binding after a host connects.
hack.meta: Common exam topic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: M=1 triggers stateful, server maintains binding table.

1.8.f.info:
The M-flag (Managed Address Configuration) is a bit in the RA that tells hosts: 'Use DHCPv6 to obtain your IPv6 address.' M=1 triggers stateful DHCPv6. M=0 (default) means hosts should not query DHCPv6 for an address (they use SLAAC or the address is manually configured).

Behavior by M/O combination:

  • M=0, O=0: Pure SLAAC. No DHCPv6.
  • M=0, O=1: Stateless DHCPv6 (SLAAC for address, DHCPv6 for DNS).
  • M=1, O=0: Stateful DHCPv6 for address, but no explicit O — O is typically implied when M=1, though some implementations still require O=1 for DNS.
  • M=1, O=1: Fully stateful DHCPv6 with O explicit for clarity. Most common stateful config.

Cisco command: ipv6 nd managed-config-flag under the interface. Remove with no ipv6 nd managed-config-flag.

Important pairing: when M=1, you typically also want A=0 for the prefix so hosts do not simultaneously run SLAAC. This is done with ipv6 nd prefix <prefix> no-autoconfig. Without it, hosts will end up with both a SLAAC and a DHCPv6-assigned address — not always desired.

Verification: show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 shows 'ND advertised managed config flag is TRUE' when M=1.

Exam phrasing: 'Which RA flag causes a host to use DHCPv6 for addressing?' → M-flag. 'What command sets M=1 on a Cisco router?' → ipv6 nd managed-config-flag.

right.label: M-flag ON
items: Pair with A=0 to disable SLAAC

hack.memory: M = Managed (full DHCPv6 control). M=1 triggers stateful DHCPv6 for addressing. Command: ipv6 nd managed-config-flag. Pair with A=0 via no-autoconfig to disable SLAAC.
hack.practice: Draw the M-flag truth table. Configure ipv6 nd managed-config-flag and verify via show ipv6 interface. Combine with no-autoconfig and observe host behavior in Packet Tracer.
hack.meta: Classic exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23.

1.8.f.info:
SARR (Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply) is the four-step DHCPv6 exchange for stateful address assignment. It is the IPv6 analogue of DHCPv4's DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Ack) — same idea, different message names.

The four steps:

  1. Solicit — client multicasts to FF02::1:2 asking for an address. Source is the client's link-local; destination is the DHCPv6 server multicast group.
  2. Advertise — DHCPv6 server replies with an address offer and configuration options. Contains the server's DUID and the offered address.
  3. Request — client formally asks for the offered address (necessary because multiple servers might have Advertised, and the client picks one).
  4. Reply — chosen server confirms the assignment. Client now has the address with a valid lease.

Compared to DHCPv4 DORA:

  • DHCPv4: Discover (broadcast) → Offer → Request (broadcast) → Ack
  • DHCPv6: Solicit (multicast FF02::1:2) → Advertise → Request (multicast) → Reply

Same 4-step handshake, different transport (multicast vs broadcast) and naming.

Optional rapid-commit: DHCPv6 supports a 2-step exchange (Solicit + Reply) if the client requests rapid commit in the Solicit and the server supports it. This cuts latency but skips the multi-server comparison.

Renewal: after initial assignment, clients renew with unicast Renew (to server) → Reply. Rebind is used if renewal fails.

hack.memory: SARR = Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply. IPv6 version of DORA. Four steps, same as DHCPv4 conceptually. Use FF02::1:2 for client → server multicasts.
hack.practice: Flashcard SARR steps. Compare side by side with DORA. In Wireshark, capture a DHCPv6 exchange and identify all four messages.
hack.meta: Common exam fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Memorize SARR order.

1.8.f.info:
Even with stateful DHCPv6, the default gateway still comes from the Router Advertisement — NOT from DHCPv6. This is a critical difference from IPv4 DHCP, which includes the default gateway in its DHCP Offer. DHCPv6 has no equivalent option; gateway learning is always an NDP function.

How the gateway is learned: the host receives an RA from a router. The RA's source address (the router's link-local) is installed as the default gateway. Even if DHCPv6 assigns the host's global address, the gateway does not come from the DHCPv6 Reply.

Implication: if you use stateful DHCPv6 but block or filter RAs (e.g., RA Guard misconfigured), hosts get an address but no gateway — they can talk locally but cannot reach other subnets. Classic troubleshooting scenario.

Why IPv6 chose this design: the RA is already essential for IPv6 (carries on-link prefix, MTU, and required for NDP functionality). Centralizing gateway info in the RA keeps NDP as the one source of truth. DHCPv6 focuses on address + optional config, not topology discovery.

Exam trap: question like 'A host uses stateful DHCPv6. Where does it learn its default gateway?' The correct answer is 'from the RA,' not 'from the DHCPv6 server.' Many students miss this because they extrapolate from DHCPv4 behavior.

Verification on a Windows host: ipconfig /all shows both the DHCPv6-assigned address and the default gateway (which came from the RA). On Linux: ip -6 route shows the default route with the router's link-local as next-hop.

children.name: Address — SLAAC or DHCPv6 (depending on flags)
Default Gateway — ALWAYS from RA (router's FE80::)

hack.memory: Gateway comes from the RA, NOT DHCPv6. Even in stateful DHCPv6 mode. Address might come from DHCPv6, but the gateway is the RA source (router's link-local). Classic exam gotcha.
hack.practice: Troubleshoot scenario: stateful DHCPv6 works, hosts get addresses, but cannot reach other subnets. Check: are RAs being received? If blocked, hosts have no gateway.
hack.meta: Very common exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Memorize: gateway = RA, always.

1.8.f.info:
Every IPv6-enabled interface automatically generates a link-local address (FE80::/10) regardless of which addressing method is used for the global address. Link-local is always there — SLAAC, stateful DHCPv6, static configuration, or pure manual assignment all coexist with the automatic link-local.

How link-local is generated: the host takes FE80::/64 as the prefix and derives the interface ID via EUI-64 (older) or RFC 7217 (modern). The result is something like FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E. DAD runs on this address before it is claimed.

Why link-local is required:

  • NDP messages (RS, RA, NS, NA) require link-local source addresses.
  • Routing protocols (OSPFv3, EIGRP for IPv6) form adjacencies using link-local addresses.
  • Default gateway on hosts is the router's link-local, not global.
  • DHCPv6 client exchanges use link-local as source.

Coexistence example: a host with stateful DHCPv6 will have:

  • FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E/64 — auto-generated link-local
  • 2001:DB8:1::100/128 — DHCPv6-assigned global

Both exist simultaneously. The link-local is used for NDP and the gateway's link-local; the global is used for internet communication.

Manual override: you can override the auto-generated link-local with ipv6 address FE80::1 link-local — useful on routers to make the link-local memorable (FE80::1 instead of a long EUI-64 derived value).

hack.memory: Link-local = always auto. Every IPv6 interface gets a FE80:: address, regardless of SLAAC/DHCPv6/static. Required for NDP, routing protocols, and gateway discovery.
hack.practice: Run show ipv6 interface on any IPv6-enabled router interface. You will see both the link-local (FE80::) and the global/configured address. Prove to yourself the link-local is independent.
hack.meta: Fundamental IPv6 fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know: link-local is always auto-generated, always required.

1.8.f.info:
ipv6 nd managed-config-flag is the Cisco interface command that sets M=1 in outgoing RAs, signaling hosts to use stateful DHCPv6 for address assignment.

Full stateful DHCPv6 config sequence:

ipv6 dhcp pool STATEFUL
 address prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64 lifetime infinite infinite
 dns-server 2001:DB8::53
 domain-name example.com
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
 ipv6 nd managed-config-flag
 ipv6 nd prefix 2001:DB8:1::/64 no-autoconfig
 ipv6 dhcp server STATEFUL
 no shutdown

Four critical pieces:

  1. Pool — defines address range, DNS, domain
  2. M-flag set via ipv6 nd managed-config-flag
  3. A-flag cleared via ipv6 nd prefix ... no-autoconfig — prevents hosts from running SLAAC alongside
  4. Server enabled via ipv6 dhcp server — applies the pool to the interface

If you skip the no-autoconfig: hosts run both SLAAC AND stateful DHCPv6, ending up with two global addresses. Usually not desired.

Verification:

  • show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 — confirms 'ND advertised managed config flag is TRUE'
  • show ipv6 dhcp binding — shows clients' leases
  • show ipv6 dhcp pool — pool status and utilization

Common mistakes: forgetting no-autoconfig (dual addresses), forgetting ipv6 dhcp server (interface does not serve the pool), forgetting ipv6 unicast-routing globally (router does not forward).

hack.memory: ipv6 nd managed-config-flag = M=1. Pair with no-autoconfig on the prefix to clear A-flag and avoid dual addresses. Then ipv6 dhcp server to apply the pool.
hack.practice: Build a stateful DHCPv6 lab. Verify show ipv6 interface shows M=TRUE. Check show ipv6 dhcp binding after a PC connects.
hack.meta: Configuration exam topic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23.

1.8.g.info:
NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol, RFC 4861) is the IPv6 protocol that replaces multiple IPv4 functions: ARP (address resolution), ICMP Router Discovery (finding default gateways), and ICMP Redirect (routing hints). NDP uses ICMPv6 messages, not a separate layer-2 protocol.

Five NDP message types:

  • RS (Router Solicitation) — Type 133. Host asks routers to send an RA.
  • RA (Router Advertisement) — Type 134. Router announces prefix, gateway, flags.
  • NS (Neighbor Solicitation) — Type 135. Resolves IPv6 → MAC, also used for DAD.
  • NA (Neighbor Advertisement) — Type 136. Reply to NS, carries MAC.
  • Redirect — Type 137. Router informs host of a better next-hop.

Compared to IPv4 equivalents:

  • ARP Request/Reply → NS/NA (Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement)
  • ICMP Router Advertisement (rarely used in IPv4) → RS/RA (core to IPv6)
  • ICMP Redirect → Redirect (same concept)

Key advantages over ARP:

  • Multicast instead of broadcast — NS goes to solicited-node multicast (FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx), not all-ports flood.
  • Integrated with router discovery — one protocol family handles both layer-3 neighbors and gateway learning.
  • DAD built in — every address validates uniqueness before use.
  • Extensible — new options can be added to RA/NS/NA without protocol changes.

Neighbor cache: the IPv6 equivalent of the ARP table. View with show ipv6 neighbors. Entries have states: INCMP (incomplete), REACH (reachable), STALE, DELAY, PROBE.

hack.memory: NDP = ARP + Router Discovery + Redirect, all in one ICMPv6 protocol. Five messages: RS/RA for routers, NS/NA for neighbors, Redirect for hints. Uses multicast, not broadcast. More efficient than ARP.
hack.practice: Flashcard all 5 messages with their ICMPv6 type numbers. Capture NDP traffic in Wireshark to see them in action. Run show ipv6 neighbors on a router.
hack.meta: Core IPv6 topic. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Must know all five message types.

1.8.g.info:
Router Solicitation (RS) is ICMPv6 Type 133 — sent by a host to ask any routers on the link to send an RA. This jumpstarts the autoconfiguration process instead of waiting for the next periodic RA.

Packet characteristics:

  • Type: 133
  • Code: 0
  • Destination: FF02::2 (all-routers multicast, link-local scope)
  • Source: host's link-local address, or :: if not yet generated
  • Payload: optional source link-layer address option (the host's MAC)

When sent:

  • Interface comes up (boot, reconnect, wake from sleep)
  • Host actively needs configuration refresh

Rate-limited: a host sends at most 3 RS messages (default) 4 seconds apart to avoid flooding. After that, it waits for unsolicited RAs.

Router response: each router on the link replies with its own RA. Responses may be unicast to the soliciting host or multicast to FF02::1, depending on the router's policy.

Contrast with IPv4: IPv4 has no equivalent. Gateway discovery in IPv4 relies on DHCP (which provides the default gateway option) or static configuration. IPv6's RS/RA mechanism is more protocol-native.

hack.memory: RS = Type 133 = 'Routers, anyone?' Sent to FF02::2 (all routers). Response triggers RA. Rate-limited: max 3 per boot cycle.
hack.practice: Flashcard: RS=133, destination=FF02::2. Wireshark a Linux boot — the first NDP packet from the host should be an RS.
hack.meta: Required memorization. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. RS=133, FF02::2.

1.8.g.info:
Router Advertisement (RA) is ICMPv6 Type 134 — sent by routers to announce their presence, advertise prefixes, and convey autoconfiguration flags. RAs are the heart of IPv6 host configuration.

Packet characteristics:

  • Type: 134
  • Code: 0
  • Destination: FF02::1 (all-nodes multicast) for periodic RAs; unicast to the requester for RS responses
  • Source: router's link-local address (FE80::) — this is what hosts install as the default gateway

Payload fields:

  • Cur Hop Limit — default TTL hosts should use
  • M-flag, O-flag — managed/other config flags
  • Router Lifetime — how long this router is a valid default gateway (seconds)
  • Reachable Time — NDP reachability timer
  • Retrans Timer — retransmit interval for NS
  • Options: Source Link-Layer Address, MTU, Prefix Information (prefix, prefix length, A/L flags, valid/preferred lifetimes), RDNSS

Timing: Cisco default periodic interval is random between ~200 sec (max) and 0.5× (min). Tunable with ipv6 nd ra interval [max] [min].

Disabling RAs: on a host-facing interface where you do not want SLAAC, use ipv6 nd ra suppress. On infrastructure links (router-to-router), you often suppress RAs.

nodes: Router periodic timer or RS received

hack.memory: RA = Type 134 = router announcement. Sent to FF02::1 periodically or to host in response to RS. Default gateway = RA source (router's FE80::). Carries prefix, flags (A/M/O), lifetimes.
hack.practice: show ipv6 interface on a Cisco router displays RA interval, lifetime, prefix advertised. Capture RAs in Wireshark and inspect all fields.
hack.meta: Core NDP message. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Know type=134, destination=FF02::1, source=router's FE80::.

1.8.g.info:
Neighbor Solicitation (NS) is ICMPv6 Type 135. NS serves two distinct purposes: address resolution (IPv6 → MAC, replacing ARP Request) and Duplicate Address Detection (DAD).

For address resolution:

  • Destination: solicited-node multicast of the target (FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx, where xx:xxxx = last 24 bits of target's unicast address)
  • Source: querier's link-local or global unicast
  • Target Address option: the IPv6 address being resolved
  • Source Link-Layer Address option: the querier's MAC (so target can reply directly)

Target replies with NA (Type 136) containing its MAC. Requester stores the mapping in the neighbor cache.

For DAD:

  • Destination: solicited-node multicast of the tentative address
  • Source: :: (unspecified — host has not claimed any address yet)
  • Target Address: the tentative address being tested
  • No Source Link-Layer Address option (because source is ::)

If any node responds with NA, the tentative address is a duplicate and must be abandoned. No response = unique = safe to use.

Why multicast instead of broadcast? NS goes only to the solicited-node group (derived from the target's last 24 bits). Typically, only the target (and rare collisions) are in that group. Compare to ARP's broadcast, which forces every NIC on the subnet to process the frame up to L2.

Exam-critical packet details: NS for address resolution has a real source address; NS for DAD has :: as source. Destination is always the solicited-node multicast.

hack.memory: NS = Type 135 = Neighbor Solicit = 'who has this address?' (like ARP Request). Dest = solicited-node multicast. Also used for DAD with source ::. NS + NA replace ARP Request + Reply.
hack.practice: Flashcard NS fields. In Wireshark, capture a ping between two IPv6 hosts — the first exchange should be NS/NA. Note the solicited-node destination.
hack.meta: Core exam fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23.

1.8.g.info:
Neighbor Advertisement (NA) is ICMPv6 Type 136, the reply to an NS. It carries the target's link-layer address (MAC) back to the requester, completing the IPv6 → MAC resolution — the replacement for ARP Reply.

Packet characteristics:

  • Type: 136
  • Destination: typically the requester's unicast address (learned from the NS source)
  • Source: the target's own unicast address
  • Target Address option: the address being advertised
  • Target Link-Layer Address option: the target's MAC
  • Flags (R, S, O):
    • R = Router (sender is a router)
    • S = Solicited (this NA is a response to an NS, not unsolicited)
    • O = Override (update existing neighbor cache entry)

Unsolicited NA: sent without a prior NS when a host's MAC changes (e.g., VRRP failover, NIC replacement). Destination is typically FF02::1 (all-nodes) with O=1 to tell everyone to overwrite their neighbor cache.

For DAD: if a host responds to a DAD NS with an NA, it signals to the tentative host that the address is already in use. The tentative host abandons the address.

Exam key points:

  • NA = Type 136
  • Replaces ARP Reply
  • Flags: R, S, O
  • Unsolicited NA to FF02::1 announces a MAC change

hack.memory: NA = Type 136 = Neighbor Advertisement = 'I'm here, here's my MAC' (like ARP Reply). Flags R/S/O: Router, Solicited, Override. Unsolicited NA announces MAC changes.
hack.practice: Flashcard NA fields and flags. In Wireshark, inspect an NA and identify the Target Address and Target Link-Layer Address options.
hack.meta: Routine exam fact. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. NA=136, replaces ARP Reply.

1.8.g.info:
The solicited-node multicast address is a per-unicast-address multicast group that optimizes NDP's neighbor discovery. It is formed as FF02::1:FF + last 24 bits of the unicast address. Every IPv6 interface automatically joins the solicited-node groups for each of its unicast addresses.

Construction:

  1. Take the unicast address, e.g. 2001:DB8::1234:5678.
  2. Extract the last 24 bits (6 hex digits): 34:5678.
  3. Prefix with FF02::1:FF: FF02::1:FF34:5678.

More examples:

  • 2001:DB8::1 → FF02::1:FF00:0001 = FF02::1:FF00:1
  • FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E → FF02::1:FF3C:4D5E
  • 2001:DB8:CAFE:1:ABCD:EF12:3456:789A → FF02::1:FF56:789A

Why this design? Instead of flooding neighbor solicitations to every device (like ARP), NS messages target a small, specific group. Typically only one device on the link is in any given solicited-node group — the owner of that address. Unrelated devices never process the frame above L2.

When a host joins: as soon as a host configures a unicast address (link-local, SLAAC global, static), it joins that address's solicited-node group and informs its local switch via MLD (Multicast Listener Discovery).

Used by: NS for address resolution, NS for DAD. Both send to the solicited-node group of the target address.

Prefix: FF02::1:FF00:0/104 — the first 104 bits are fixed; the last 24 bits vary.

children.name: Used by NS for lookup + DAD

hack.memory: Solicited-node = FF02::1:FF + last 24 bits of the unicast address. Every host auto-joins for each of its addresses. Used by NS. Dramatically more efficient than ARP broadcast.
hack.practice: Compute solicited-node for 5 random unicast addresses. Practice: 2001:DB8::1 → FF02::1:FF00:1. FE80::ABCD:1234 → FF02::1:FFCD:1234.
hack.meta: Common exam calculation. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Practice until automatic.

1.8.g.info:
show ipv6 neighbors displays the router's neighbor cache — the IPv6 equivalent of show arp. Each entry maps an IPv6 address to a MAC and interface, with a state showing how fresh the entry is.

Example output:

Router# show ipv6 neighbors
IPv6 Address                              Age Link-layer Addr State Interface
FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E                    5 001a.2b3c.4d5e  REACH Gi0/0
2001:DB8:1::100                             2 001a.2b3c.4d5e  REACH Gi0/0
FE80::C8E0:FFFF:FE12:3456                  30 c8e0.ff12.3456  STALE Gi0/1

Fields:

  • IPv6 Address — neighbor's address
  • Age — time since last update (seconds or minutes)
  • Link-layer Addr — neighbor's MAC
  • State — INCMP (incomplete, no reply yet), REACH (reachable, recently confirmed), STALE (not confirmed recently, may be gone), DELAY (between STALE and PROBE), PROBE (actively sending NS to verify)
  • Interface — outgoing interface to reach the neighbor

Neighbor cache states (simplified):

  • INCOMPLETE — NS sent, awaiting NA
  • REACHABLE — confirmed reachable within Reachable Time (default 30 sec)
  • STALE — timer expired, use if still working but verify on next transaction
  • DELAY — waiting a few seconds before probing
  • PROBE — sending NS to verify; if no reply, entry is removed

Related commands:

  • clear ipv6 neighbors — flush the cache (rarely needed)
  • ipv6 neighbor 2001:DB8::5 Gi0/0 0011.2233.4455 — add a static entry

hack.memory: show ipv6 neighbors = show arp for IPv6. Displays neighbor cache with state machine (REACH, STALE, PROBE). IPv6 tracks reachability more actively than IPv4 ARP.
hack.practice: On a Cisco router, run show ipv6 neighbors. Identify the state column. Clear the cache and watch entries rebuild. Compare output with show arp.
hack.meta: Verification command. Jeremy IT Lab Day 32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Equivalent of show arp for IPv6.

1.8.h.info:
ipv6 unicast-routing is the global configuration command that enables the router to forward IPv6 packets between interfaces and send Router Advertisements.
