Without it, the router behaves like a host for IPv6 — it can receive and originate IPv6 traffic on its own interfaces, but it will not route between them and will not advertise prefixes to hosts. This is the single most forgotten command in IPv6 labs.

Behavior without the command:

  • Router interfaces can have IPv6 addresses assigned.
  • Router can ping its own IPv6 interfaces.
  • Router does NOT route IPv6 between interfaces.
  • Router does NOT send RAs.
  • Hosts on LANs do NOT autoconfigure via SLAAC.

Behavior with the command enabled:

  • Router forwards IPv6 unicast packets per the IPv6 routing table.
  • Router sends periodic RAs on IPv6-enabled interfaces (default every ~200 sec).
  • Interfaces join all-routers multicast (FF02::2).
  • Routing protocols (OSPFv3, EIGRP for IPv6) can form neighbor relationships.

Configuration:

Router(config)# ipv6 unicast-routing

That is the entire command. Global mode, one line, done.

Verification:

  • show running-config | include ipv6 unicast-routing — confirms it is enabled
  • show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 — if the router is forwarding, the output shows RA settings; if not, RAs are not being sent

Exam trap: troubleshooting question — 'IPv6 is configured on all interfaces, hosts have addresses, but cannot reach other subnets.' First check: ipv6 unicast-routing. It is almost always the missing command.

states: ipv6 unicast-routing OFF: router acts as host
ipv6 unicast-routing ON: router forwards + sends RAs

hack.memory: ipv6 unicast-routing = master switch for IPv6 routing. Without it, router does NOT forward IPv6 or send RAs. #1 forgotten command in IPv6 labs. When IPv6 'is not working,' check this first.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure IPv6 on two interfaces WITHOUT ipv6 unicast-routing. Try to ping from one LAN to the other — fails. Add ipv6 unicast-routing and retry — works. This 30-second lab cements the lesson.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam question. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.

1.8.h.info:
ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64 is the interface command that assigns a global unicast IPv6 address. Unlike IPv4, this one command does two things:

  1. Assigns the global address — 2001:DB8:1::1 with /64 prefix.
  2. Auto-enables IPv6 on the interface — triggers link-local generation if one does not exist.

Full example:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
 no shutdown

After this, show ipv6 interface brief shows:

GigabitEthernet0/0   [up/up]
    FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E      (auto link-local)
    2001:DB8:1::1                  (configured)

Both addresses are active. The link-local is auto-generated via EUI-64 unless overridden.

Variants:

  • ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::/64 eui-64 — assigns a global address using the prefix + EUI-64 interface ID (for labs where you want to demonstrate EUI-64)
  • ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64 anycast — marks the address as anycast (same address on multiple devices)
  • ipv6 address autoconfig — lets the router itself use SLAAC (rare; typically used on stub routers)

Routing table effect: assigning an address produces two routes in show ipv6 route:

  • C 2001:DB8:1::/64 — connected network route
  • L 2001:DB8:1::1/128 — local host route (for the router itself)

hack.memory: ipv6 address ... /64 assigns the global AND auto-enables IPv6 on the interface. Link-local is created automatically. Produces C + L routes, just like IPv4.
hack.practice: Configure ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64 on a router interface. Run show ipv6 interface brief — verify both global and link-local addresses show. Check show ipv6 route for C and L entries.
hack.meta: Routine configuration. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.

1.8.h.info:
ipv6 enable enables IPv6 on an interface without assigning a global address. It triggers automatic link-local generation (FE80::) and lets the interface participate in IPv6 at the link scope only. Use it when the interface needs IPv6 connectivity for local-only purposes (routing protocol adjacency, NDP) but no global address.

When to use:

  • Router-to-router links where both sides use link-local only (rare, but possible)
  • Management interfaces that talk to local-link NMS
  • Lab scenarios testing pure link-local communication
  • Before assigning a global address (as a placeholder to verify link-local works)

Example:

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ipv6 enable
 no shutdown

After this, show ipv6 interface brief shows only the link-local:

GigabitEthernet0/1  [up/up]
    FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5F

Difference from ipv6 address:

  • ipv6 address <global> /<prefix> — assigns a global and auto-generates link-local
  • ipv6 enable — no global, only link-local

Not needed when you already configured a global address: any ipv6 address command implicitly enables IPv6 on the interface. ipv6 enable is redundant in that case.

Common use in routing protocols: OSPFv3 and EIGRP for IPv6 form neighbors using link-local addresses. On pure infrastructure links without global addresses, ipv6 enable suffices for routing protocols to work.

hack.memory: ipv6 enable = 'turn on IPv6 with link-local only, no global.' Useful for router-to-router links or when you only need NDP/routing protocols.
hack.practice: Configure ipv6 enable on a router interface. Verify show ipv6 interface brief shows only link-local. Note that no global appears.
hack.meta: Less common but tested. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.

1.8.h.info:
ipv6 address FE80::1 link-local manually overrides the auto-generated link-local address with a memorable value. Instead of the EUI-64-derived FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E, you can set a clean FE80::1 that is easy to type and reference.

Why override link-local?

  • Memorable gateway addresses — hosts on the LAN will learn the router's link-local as their default gateway. FE80::1 is vastly easier to remember and troubleshoot than a 16-character EUI-64 value.
  • Router ID consistency — in OSPFv3 labs, identical link-local addresses across routers simplify adjacency debugging.
  • Documentation — a network diagram showing all router link-locals as FE80::1 is much cleaner.

Example:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address FE80::1 link-local
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
 no shutdown

After this, show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 shows link-local as FE80::1 (not the auto value).

Caveats:

  • Link-local addresses must start with FE80::/10 — the first 10 bits are fixed.
  • Each interface must have a unique link-local address. You cannot set FE80::1 on two interfaces of the same router simultaneously (they would collide during DAD).
  • DAD still runs — if another host on the link is using FE80::1, the address will be rejected.

Best practice: use different memorable values per router (R1 = FE80::1, R2 = FE80::2, etc.) to avoid confusion.

hack.memory: ipv6 address FE80::1 link-local = override the ugly EUI-64 link-local with something memorable. Good practice for router gateway addresses.
hack.practice: Configure FE80::1 as link-local on R1. Then set FE80::2 on R2 with the same command. Verify on connected PCs that the default gateway is FE80::1 — much easier to read than the auto value.
hack.meta: Common lab practice. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31-32. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.

1.8.h.info:
show ipv6 interface brief is the IPv6 equivalent of show ip interface brief — a quick overview of every interface, its IPv6 addresses, and its operational status.

Example output:

Router# show ipv6 interface brief
GigabitEthernet0/0     [up/up]
    FE80::1
    2001:DB8:1::1
GigabitEthernet0/1     [up/up]
    FE80::21A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5F
    2001:DB8:2::1
GigabitEthernet0/2     [administratively down/down]
    unassigned
Loopback0              [up/up]
    FE80::1:1
    2001:DB8:0:0::1

Fields:

  • Interface name
  • [Line protocol/Admin status] — [up/up], [down/down], [administratively down/down]
  • IPv6 addresses — all addresses assigned (link-local + global + any anycast)

What to look for:

  • Both a link-local (FE80::) AND a global/configured address per active interface
  • Status should be [up/up] for a working interface
  • 'unassigned' means no IPv6 addresses — either interface is not configured for IPv6, or only ipv6 enable is applied with link-local pending

Use cases:

  • Quick verification after configuration — did my IPv6 address apply?
  • Troubleshooting — is the interface up? Does it have both link-local and global?
  • Inventory — what IPv6 addresses are assigned across the router?

Related commands:

  • show ipv6 interface Gi0/0 — detailed view of one interface (RA settings, flags, multicast groups joined, MTU)
  • show ipv6 route — the routing table

hack.memory: show ipv6 interface brief = quick IPv6 inventory. Every interface, every IPv6 address, up/up status. Expect to see both link-local (FE80::) and global per active interface.
hack.practice: Run show ipv6 interface brief on any Cisco router with IPv6 configured. Confirm you see link-local AND global addresses. Shut an interface and re-run — watch address disappear.
hack.meta: Standard verification command. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.

1.8.h.info:
show ipv6 route displays the IPv6 routing table. Structure is identical to show ip route — a list of known destination networks with source code, prefix, administrative distance, metric, next-hop, and exit interface.

Example output:

Router# show ipv6 route
IPv6 Routing Table - default - 5 entries
Codes: C - Connected, L - Local, S - Static, U - Per-user Static route
       B - BGP, R - RIP, I1 - ISIS L1, I2 - ISIS L2
       IA - ISIS interarea, IS - ISIS summary, D - EIGRP
       EX - EIGRP external, O - OSPF Intra, OI - OSPF Inter
       OE1 - OSPF ext 1, OE2 - OSPF ext 2, ON1 - OSPF NSSA ext 1
       ON2 - OSPF NSSA ext 2
C   2001:DB8:1::/64 [0/0]
     via GigabitEthernet0/0, directly connected
L   2001:DB8:1::1/128 [0/0]
     via GigabitEthernet0/0, receive
S   2001:DB8:2::/64 [1/0]
     via 2001:DB8:1::2
O   2001:DB8:3::/64 [110/20]
     via FE80::2, GigabitEthernet0/0
L   FF00::/8 [0/0]
     via Null0, receive

Key codes:

  • C — Connected (auto-installed when interface comes up with an IPv6 address)
  • L — Local (a /128 for the router's own interface address)
  • S — Static (manually configured with ipv6 route)
  • O — OSPF intra-area (OSPFv3)
  • OI — OSPF inter-area
  • D — EIGRP for IPv6
  • B — BGP

Next-hop via link-local: OSPFv3 and EIGRP for IPv6 use the neighbor's link-local address (FE80::) as the next-hop, not a global address. Static routes may use either.

Useful filters:

  • show ipv6 route connected
  • show ipv6 route static
  • show ipv6 route ospf
  • show ipv6 route 2001:DB8:3:: — best match for a specific destination

left.label: show ip route (IPv4)
items: Codes: C, L, S, O, D, B, R

hack.memory: show ipv6 route = IPv6 routing table. Same codes as IPv4 (C, L, S, O, D). OSPFv3 next-hops use link-local addresses (FE80::) — expect to see that in the via column.
hack.practice: Build a 2-router IPv6 lab with OSPFv3. Run show ipv6 route — verify O routes with FE80:: next-hops. Compare with static route next-hops (global).
hack.meta: Routine verification. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31-34. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.

1.8.h.info:
ping ipv6-address tests IPv6 reachability the same way ping tests IPv4. Cisco IOS auto-detects IPv6 addresses. On hosts, the command is typically ping (modern OSes) or ping6 (older Linux).

Examples:

Router# ping 2001:DB8:2::1
Type escape sequence to abort.
Sending 5, 100-byte ICMP Echos to 2001:DB8:2::1, timeout is 2 seconds:
!!!!!
Success rate is 100 percent (5/5), round-trip min/avg/max = 1/1/4 ms

Pinging link-local addresses: since link-local is only valid per link, you must specify the interface:

Router# ping FE80::1
Output Interface: GigabitEthernet0/0

Or on Linux: ping FE80::1%eth0 (the %eth0 is the zone identifier).

Extended ping: ping ipv6 (no address) enters interactive mode — specify target, source, repeat count, datagram size, timeout.

Exit codes and symbols:

  • ! — reply received (success)
  • . — timeout (no reply within 2 sec)
  • U — destination unreachable (ICMPv6 Type 1)
  • H — host unreachable
  • T — TTL exceeded

Common troubleshooting flow:

  1. Ping loopback (::1) — verifies IPv6 stack works locally.
  2. Ping own interface address — verifies interface is up/up.
  3. Ping link-local of directly connected router — verifies L2 connectivity.
  4. Ping a remote address — tests full routing path.

hack.memory: ping <v6 address> = test IPv6 reachability. For link-local, specify interface (interactive mode or %eth0). Standard Cisco escalation: ::1 → own IP → neighbor → remote.
hack.practice: Ping ::1 on a router — should succeed. Ping a directly connected neighbor's link-local — requires interface. Ping a remote global — requires full routing.
hack.meta: Basic verification. Jeremy IT Lab Day 31. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 24.
